title: SEGMENT 7 — GlymaxX and alternative afucose platforms

lines.text: SEGMENT 7 — GlymaxX and alternative afucose platforms
Potelligent was first, but it wasn't the only game in town for very long.
Within a few years of the Kyowa Hakko discovery, several other companies developed alternative platforms for producing afucosylated antibodies, each with its own engineering twist.
The most successful of these alternatives is GlymaxX, developed by a Berlin-based company called ProBioGen.
And I want to spend this segment on GlymaxX because the approach is conceptually different from FUT8 knockout, and the contrast is instructive.
Here's the FUT8-knockout strategy in one sentence: remove the enzyme that adds fucose.
Here's the GlymaxX strategy in one sentence: starve the cell of fucose substrate.
Two different ways to skin the same biochemical cat, and both end up with the same product — an antibody with no core fucose.
Let me unpack the GlymaxX biology.
To add core fucose to a glycan, you need three things: a fucose-adding enzyme (FUT8), a Golgi-localized substrate (GDP-fucose), and the glycan acceptor (the bottom GlcNAc of the IgG glycan).
FUT8 knockout removes the first.
GlymaxX attacks the second — the GDP-fucose substrate — by interfering with its biosynthesis.
GDP-fucose is made in the cytoplasm by a two-enzyme pathway.
GMD — GDP-mannose 4,6-dehydratase — converts GDP-mannose to an intermediate.
Then FX — also called GFUS — completes the conversion to GDP-fucose.
The GDP-fucose is then imported into the Golgi by a specific transporter and used by FUT8 (and other fucosyltransferases) to fucosylate substrates.
What ProBioGen did was clever.
Instead of knocking out FUT8 directly, they transfected CHO cells with a gene encoding a bacterial enzyme called RMD — GDP-6-deoxy-D-lyxo-4-hexulose reductase.
RMD diverts GDP-mannose down a different pathway, away from the GDP-fucose pathway.
It eats up the substrate that would otherwise have been turned into GDP-fucose.
So the cell still has its FUT8 enzyme, fully intact, but there's no GDP-fucose for FUT8 to use.
No substrate, no product.
The antibody comes out afucosylated.
This is a substrate-depletion strategy.
It's a different point of intervention in the same metabolic pathway.
And it has a few practical advantages over the knockout approach.
First, you don't have to disrupt an endogenous gene — you just add a foreign gene.
That means you can apply GlymaxX as a retrofitting technology to existing CHO lines that have already been developed for a particular antibody.
You don't have to start over from scratch with a new host line; you can convert an existing line.
That's a big deal for companies who have invested years in characterizing a particular CHO clone.
Second, the conversion is fast.
You can transfect a CHO line with RMD, select for stable expression, and within weeks you have a clone that produces afucosylated antibody.
Compared to the months or years of gene-targeting work originally needed to make FUT8 knockouts, that's an acceleration.
Third, the GlymaxX engineering is gene-additive rather than gene-disruptive, which some regulators have historically found slightly easier to characterize.
The trade-off is that GlymaxX requires you to introduce a foreign protein into your production cell.
RMD is bacterial in origin, and the cells now express it.
Regulators care about this — they want to know that the bacterial enzyme doesn't co-purify with the antibody, doesn't trigger immunogenicity, doesn't have off-pathway effects on the cell.

ProBioGen has done extensive characterization to address these concerns, and GlymaxX has been used to produce multiple clinical-stage antibodies, including afucosylated drugs that have entered late-stage trials.
The regulatory acceptance is well-established at this point.
There are other afucose platforms beyond Potelligent and GlymaxX.
Lonza's GS-XCeed platform has internal afucose options.
Catalent, through its Redwood and other acquisitions, offers afucose engineering.
Some companies have engineered alternative cell lines — duck cells, rat cells, plant cells — that naturally produce afucosylated glycoproteins because they lack FUT8-like enzymes.
Each platform has its niche.
But conceptually, the field has converged on two main strategies: remove the enzyme, or starve the substrate.
Potelligent and GlymaxX are the canonical examples of each.
And once these platforms existed, the next question was: which antibody do we make first?
Which therapeutic indication, which target, which patient population, will best showcase the afucose advantage?
That brings us to mogamulizumab.
